content.text: callback returns a function, React treats that function as the effect's

. React calls it in two situations: just before the component unmounts, and just before re-running the same effect when a dependency has changed. Cleanup is how React prevents memory leaks, stale callbacks, and duplicate subscriptions from accumulating across renders.

The diagram shows the three moments cleanup fires: before each re-run (dependency changed), on unmount, and — in strict mode — once extra on mount to verify your effect is resilient to being torn down and restarted.
The return value of the effect callback must be either a function (the cleanup) or nothing (
). Returning any other value — a Promise, a number, an object — causes a runtime warning in development and is undefined behaviour in production.
The async-effect mistake is extremely common. Fix it by declaring an async function
the callback and calling it immediately, so the outer callback remains synchronous and can still return a cleanup:
React fires cleanup at predictable, well-defined moments. Understanding the exact order eliminates an entire class of concurrency bugs.
When a component is removed from the tree, React runs the cleanup of every registered effect. This is the equivalent of
When a dependency in the array changes across renders, React first calls the previous cleanup, then re-runs the effect. This ensures that each render's effect uses only fresh values.

In development with React Strict Mode, React mounts each component, immediately unmounts it (running cleanup), then remounts it. This stress-tests whether your cleanup is correct. If something breaks in strict mode that works in production, the cleanup is incomplete.
Always capture the timer ID in a variable and clear it in the cleanup. Without cleanup, the callback fires even after the component is gone, which triggers a state update on an unmounted component.
Every effect that acquires an external resource needs a corresponding release call. The table below maps common acquisitions to their cleanup:
The cleanup function captures variables from the
in which the effect was created, not from the render in which cleanup runs. This is usually what you want — the cleanup for an effect that used a specific

Stale closures become a problem when you use refs or mutable state inside cleanup and expect them to reflect the latest values. If you need the cleanup to see the latest version of a value, store it in a ref:
Omitting cleanup is not just a performance issue — it causes correctness bugs that are hard to reproduce because they depend on mount/unmount timing.
Event listeners, observers, and subscriptions accumulate without bound as the component mounts and unmounts in a long session.
An async callback (fetch, timer) fires after the component unmounts and calls setState, triggering a React warning and potentially updating stale state.
Without cleanup, every re-render with changing deps opens a new connection while leaving the old one open. A busy component can open dozens of sockets.
Without AbortController, a slow fetch for page A can resolve after the user navigates to page B and update page B's state with page A's data.
if the effect calls a function that has a corresponding 'undo' or 'cancel' function, the cleanup must call it.
triggers cleanup, making it straightforward to verify that resources are released:
internally handle their own cleanup. Callers do not need to do anything extra — React walks the entire effect list for the component tree.
Encapsulating effects and their cleanup in custom hooks keeps component code focused on rendering logic, and makes the resource lifecycle reusable and testable in isolation.
call as a separate effect slot — one slot per call site, in hook call order. Each slot maintains its own cleanup function independently. A component with three
calls has three independent cleanup slots.
Splitting effects by concern is better than combining unrelated side effects into one large
. Independent effects run and clean up independently, so a change in one dependency does not trigger unnecessary cleanup for an unrelated resource.
When a subtree unmounts, React runs cleanups in

order — the opposite of how effects originally ran. This mirrors the principle that inner components should be fully torn down before their parents, preventing a parent teardown from destroying resources a child cleanup still needs.
Cleanup is not a substitute for
. The distinction matters when choosing where to place a side-effect's counterpart:
Release a resource the effect acquired
Cancel a user-initiated async operation (e.g. form submit)
track a ref inside the handler, not an effect
Un-register a listener the component added on mount
Notify a server when the user clicks 'leave'
Cleanup is reactive — React calls it automatically based on the lifecycle. Event handler teardown is
— the user action drives it. Using cleanup to respond to user events leads to code that is hard to follow and easy to get wrong.

. It is not a place to handle user intent. If you find yourself wanting cleanup to do something visible to the user (show a confirmation, send a message), that logic belongs in an event handler instead.

The cleanup function is the mechanism React provides for releasing resources that an effect acquires. Return it as a function from the effect callback, and React will call it on unmount and before every re-run triggered by a dependency change.
Return a function from the effect callback to register cleanup.
Cleanup fires before unmount and before each re-run (dep changed).
Strict Mode runs an extra cleanup+remount cycle in development to expose missing cleanup.
Async effects: declare the async function inside the callback; return a cancellation cleanup.
Common cleanup calls: clearTimeout, clearInterval, removeEventListener, controller.abort(), ws.close(), subscription.unsubscribe().
Cleanup closures capture values from the render that created the effect — by design.
Missing cleanup causes memory leaks, duplicate subscriptions, and stale state updates.
Custom hooks that use useEffect manage their own cleanup — callers need not do anything extra.
